summary: Tech platforms inevitably turn on their ecosystem partners by launching competing products that are 'good enough' and bundled into their core offering. Drawing from observations at Snowflake Summit, Reis argues that vendors must diversify across platforms, build beloved products, and own their customer relationships to survive the predictable platform predation cycle.
key_points: Platforms don't just want to enable the ecosystem—they want to BE the ecosystem
Platform-launched competing products are typically 80% of dedicated vendor functionality but bundled and cheaper
Vendors should never be over-reliant on a single platform for revenue or go-to-market
Strong brand and word-of-mouth can make platform competition 'practically irrelevant'
The data space is in a 'Great Consolidation' with more M&A and platform feature expansion imminent
money_quotes: One day, the platform is your partner. The next day, they're like a wild predator trying to eat you alive.
Platforms don't just want to enable the ecosystem. They want to be the ecosystem.
In the end, every fragmented ecosystem consolidates into a monolith. The big eat the small and slow.
You can have the most fantastic product in the world, but if nobody knows about it, it won't matter very much.
The platform that is buddy-buddy with you today may try to replace you tomorrow.
key_insight: Platform partnerships are inherently unstable because platforms are incentivized to absorb successful partner functionality, making vendor diversification and brand differentiation essential survival strategies.
